steps.content: Your If-Then Plans fire here: 'If someone surges in the first 10 minutes, I will hold my pace and let them go.' 'If my heart rate is above zone 2 in the first 20 minutes, I will back off regardless of how it feels.' The decision was already made — last week, with a clear head.

blocks.prompt: Mile 3 of a 100-mile gravel race. A group of 5 riders surges 15 seconds ahead. Your legs are fresh. The chimp is screaming: don't let them go. Your race plan says hold pace for the first hour.

options.label: A
options.text: Go with them — you're fresh and strong, this might be the winning move.
options.consequence: The chimp wins. At mile 3, you have no idea if this group will hold. You've abandoned your race plan based on a 15-second gap and fresh legs. By mile 30, the effort tax from that early surge compounds. By mile 60, you're alone and paying interest on a loan you took out at mile 3.

options.label: B
options.text: Let them go, execute your pacing plan — the race is 97 miles long.
options.consequence: The coach wins. You've demonstrated exactly the discipline the first hour demands. The mantra works: 'Settle. Execute. The race is long.' At mile 60, when those riders are fading, you'll be passing them with a controlled effort and a clear head.

options.label: C
options.text: Half-chase — bridge halfway and compromise your pace to stay in visual range.
options.consequence: The worst of both worlds. You've violated your race plan AND failed to catch the group. You're now in no-man's land — faster than planned, slower than needed, and burning matches with no return. The half-commitment costs more than either full commitment.

blocks.content: Commitment: My first-hour mantra is: ___.
The race isn't won in the first hour. But it can be lost there.
Next — the moment where the race IS decided.

lessons.title: Deliver
lessons.description: This is clutch territory. Maximum deliberate effort. The moment every tool was built for.

blocks.content: There's a moment in every endurance race where the race actually happens. Not at the start. Not in the middle miles. Somewhere between 'this is hard' and 'I can't do this,' you make a decision — in ten seconds, maybe less — that determines the outcome of everything that came before it.

The chimp wakes up. It's been quiet for hours, but now it's screaming: 'This is too hard. You can't sustain this. The gap is too big. Just let it go.' And in that exact moment — with those words echoing in your skull — you have a choice.
By the end of this lesson, you will have memorized the 5-step Grit Stack and be able to deploy it under pressure.

blocks.question: In the first hour, you should trust ___ first.

options.label: C
options.text: Objective data (watch, power meter) — because adrenaline corrupts RPE early

feedback.correct: Correct. In the first hour, adrenaline subtracts 1-3 RPE points, making threshold feel like tempo. Trust data early, trust RPE later.
feedback.incorrect: In the first hour, adrenaline corrupts RPE — hard efforts feel easy. Trust objective data (watch, power meter) early. Switch to RPE as primary later in the race when adrenaline fades.

blocks.content: This is clutch territory. Not flow. Flow is gone. Clutch is what's left — the most deliberate, most conscious, most intentional thing you've ever done. Maximum deliberate effort. The moment every tool in this course was built for.

tabs.label: Association — High Intensity
tabs.content: At high intensity, the adaptive strategy is association: tune IN, monitor the body, stay connected to breathing, leg sensation, heart rate (Brick, MacIntyre, & Campbell, 2014). The deciding moment requires you to be fully, completely, brutally present with what your body is doing. You need that information to manage the effort and prevent catastrophic blowup.

tabs.label: Dissociation — Moderate Intensity
tabs.content: At moderate intensity, dissociation — letting your mind wander, not monitoring closely — reduces perceived effort and is generally adaptive. But the deciding moment is NOT moderate intensity. Dissociating during the hardest moment of your race is checking out when you most need to check in.

panels.title: Segmentation
panels.content: Breaking remaining distance into sub-goals is one of the most robustly supported coping strategies in the literature. 'I can't hold this for another hour' triggers the shutdown response. 'I can hold it to the next mile marker' doesn't. The effort is identical. The framing changes everything.

panels.title: Perceptual Reappraisal
panels.content: Pain tolerance at a fixed RPE predicted time trial performance (Astokorki & Mauger, 2017). Faster athletes didn't feel less pain — they tolerated more. 'My legs are burning' vs. 'My legs are telling me I'm working at a level most people never reach.' Same sensation. Different story. Different outcome.

panels.title: Cross-References
panels.content: The Grit Stack integrates: Clutch state research (4.2) — deliberate effort under pressure. Performance Statements (2.4) — pre-written reframes for the chimp's negative chess pieces. 6-2-7 breathing (1.4) — parasympathetic brake in 15 seconds.

blocks.content: The Grit Stack

blocks.alt: The Grit Stack — five layers stacked vertically: Breathe, Detach, Reframe, Execute, Segment
blocks.caption: Five steps. In order. Every time the deciding moment arrives.

steps.title: 1. Breathe (6-2-7)
steps.content: One cycle. Inhale 6, hold 2, exhale 7. Fifteen seconds to engage the parasympathetic brake and occupy the chimp with counting so it can't continue its catastrophic monologue. Even an imperfect 6-2-7 under maximum duress is infinitely better than none.

steps.title: 2. Detach ("That's the chimp")
steps.content: Name it. 'That's the chimp.' The screaming voice that says you can't continue? That's the chimp. The conviction you should quit? That's the chimp. You are not the chimp. 'I can't do this' hits differently with a prefix: 'The chimp says I can't do this.' One is a fact. The other is a report from an unreliable narrator.

steps.title: 3. Reframe (Performance Statement)
steps.content: Change what the sensation means. 'My legs are burning' becomes 'My legs are telling me I'm working at a level most people never reach.' 'I can't hold this pace' becomes 'I don't KNOW if I can — the only way to find out is to keep going.' Deploy your pain-response Performance Statement. The pain is real. The story changes.

steps.title: 4. Execute (Commit)
steps.content: Pick ONE technical cue. Not 'try harder.' One specific, executable action. On a bike: 'Smooth circles. Drop the heels.' Running: 'Light feet. Quick turnover.' The cue displaces catastrophic self-talk — your working memory can't hold both. You're either thinking 'I can't do this' or 'smooth circles.' Not both.

steps.title: 5. Segment (Next Checkpoint Only)
steps.content: Shrink the world. The remaining distance is too big — don't look at it. What's the next checkpoint? The next aid station, the next mile marker, the next turn. That's your race now. You're not running a marathon. You're running to the next lamp post. '40 more miles of suffering' triggers shutdown. 'The next half-mile' doesn't.

blocks.content: Commitment: I will rehearse the Grit Stack in my next hard interval session.
This is what you trained for. Not the fitness. This.
Next — fueling your head, not just your body.

lessons.title: Fueling Your Head
lessons.description: Nutritional anxiety is a distinct psychological construct — not just 'eat right.'

blocks.content: Mile 70 of a 100-mile race. You reach for your next gel. It's not there. You used your last one 20 minutes ago and forgot to restock at the last aid station. What happens next isn't in your stomach — it's in your head. 'I'm going to bonk.' That thought lands like a brick, and the cascade begins: heart rate ticks up from anxiety, breathing goes shallow, focus narrows from race execution to nutrition crisis.

Meanwhile, you probably have enough glycogen for another 30 minutes. The next aid station is in 15. The crisis is not physiological. It's psychological. But by the time your brain finishes spiraling, it WILL be physiological — because anxiety just spiked your cortisol, which is about to wreck your gut.
By the end of this lesson, you will have rehearsed your exact race-day nutrition plan in training.

blocks.question: The Grit Stack steps: Breathe → ___ → Reframe → ___ → Segment.

feedback.correct: Correct. Breathe → Detach → Reframe → Execute → Segment. The full sequence in about 30 seconds.
feedback.incorrect: The Grit Stack sequence: Breathe → Detach ('That's the chimp') → Reframe (Performance Statement) → Execute (one technical cue) → Segment (next checkpoint only).

blocks.content: Nutritional anxiety is a distinct psychological construct — not just 'eat right.' Your brain's BELIEF about your fuel state affects performance independently of your actual fuel state. You can eat perfectly and still bonk because your brain decided you weren't.

panels.title: The Bonking Cascade
panels.content: Low glucose impairs the prefrontal cortex — the coach. With the coach offline, the chimp runs unchecked, amplifying negative spirals. The bonking cascade isn't just about calories. It's a feedback loop between brain and gut, and the entry point is often psychological, not physiological.

panels.title: Stress → Cortisol → Gut → GI Distress → More Stress
panels.content: Cortisol from anxiety redirects blood away from the digestive system. Gastric emptying slows. Food sits in your stomach. You feel bloated, nauseous, crampy. You stop eating — making the eventual bonk worse. The fear of the bonk caused the bonk.

panels.title: 20-50% GI Distress Prevalence
panels.content: Between 20 and 50 percent of endurance athletes experience gut distress during competition (de Oliveira, Burini, & Jeukendrup, 2014). The kicker: psychological stress was identified as a major contributor. Not bad food choices. Stress itself.

panels.title: Nutritional Anxiety (Nutrients, 2025)
panels.content: Viribay et al. identified three failure patterns: anticipatory fear of dietary mistakes, internal negotiation between knowledge and situational trust ('What if today's different?'), and ritualization as psychological regulation — rigid routines that shatter when disrupted, because the plan was a coping mechanism, not just a nutrition strategy.

blocks.question: 'Nothing new on race day' is a psychological rule because:

options.label: A
options.text: New foods always cause stomach problems

options.label: B
options.text: Unfamiliar nutrition adds decision load and anxiety — your brain needs evidence from repeated successful practice to trust the plan

options.label: C
options.text: Your body can only absorb foods it has processed before

feedback.correct: Correct. Your BRAIN needs to be familiar with the fuel. Without repeated evidence that the plan works, the brain defaults to uncertainty — and uncertainty is where the chimp lives.
feedback.incorrect: It's not just physiological. Your brain needs evidence — from repeated, successful practice — that the nutrition plan works. Without that evidence, uncertainty triggers the chimp and the anxiety cascade begins.

blocks.content: The Fueling Rehearsal

steps.title: Build Your Exact Race Nutrition Plan
steps.content: Grams of carbs per hour, fluid intake, timing, brands, what you carry vs. what you grab at aid stations. We're not here to design it. We're here to bulletproof it psychologically.

steps.title: Execute the Exact Plan in 3 Training Sessions
steps.content: Same products. Same timing. Same quantities. Same intensity. Each successful rehearsal builds evidence: 'This plan works. I've done it before. My body handles it.' Three data points that say otherwise when the chimp whispers 'what if your stomach can't handle it.'

steps.title: Deliberately Practice a Disruption
steps.content: In one rehearsal, intentionally break the plan. Skip a gel. Delay a bottle. Then build your If-Then: 'If I miss a feeding window, I will take my next intake 15 minutes early.' 'If my stomach rejects a gel, I switch to liquid calories in small sips.' Pre-programmed responses for the most common fueling disruptions.

steps.title: On Race Day, Execute — Deploy the Grit Stack for Nutritional Crises
steps.content: When the cascade starts: Breathe (one 6-2-7). Detach ('That's the chimp catastrophizing about one missed gel'). Reframe ('I have enough fuel for the next 30 minutes. This is a hiccup, not a crisis'). Execute ('Eat what I have. Drink what I have. Keep moving'). Segment ('Next aid station. That's my only job').

blocks.content: Commitment: I will rehearse my race nutrition plan in my next 3 long sessions.
'Nothing new on race day' is psychological, not just physiological.
Next — what happens after the finish line.

lessons.title: After The Line
lessons.description: Structured debrief, not rumination. Process the race, then put it away.

blocks.content: The finish line you cross with your body is the easy one. It's the line you cross with your brain — 24 hours later, sitting with the result — that most athletes never prepare for.

Good race? The high lasts an hour, maybe two. Then a flatness settles in — bizarrely close to sadness. The thing that gave your days structure is in the rearview mirror. Bad race? The spiral starts immediately. Before you've left the finish area, the chimp has hijacked the analysis: 'I wasn't fit enough. I'm not cut out for this.' Either way, what happens in the 24 hours after matters more than most athletes realize.
By the end of this lesson, you will have a structured Post-Race Debrief framework.

blocks.question: Nutritional anxiety is:

options.label: A
options.text: Just being nervous about eating before a race

options.label: B
options.text: A distinct psychological construct that affects performance independently of actual fuel intake

options.label: C
options.text: A sign you haven't trained enough

feedback.correct: Correct. Nutritional anxiety is formally identified as a distinct psychological construct. You can eat perfectly and still suffer performance consequences from the anxiety alone.
feedback.incorrect: Nutritional anxiety is a formally identified psychological construct (Viribay et al., 2025) that affects performance independently of actual nutrition. The belief you're under-fueled has a direct performance cost even when caloric intake is adequate.

blocks.content: Structured debrief, not rumination. Reflection is purposeful analysis with a beginning, middle, and end. Rumination is repetitive, self-critical thought that circles the same drain. The difference isn't the content — it's the structure.

panels.title: Post-Event Emotional Crash
panels.content: After any major event — good or bad — athletes are psychologically vulnerable. You're physically depleted (low glycogen = low prefrontal function), emotionally raw (hours of effort strip psychological defenses), and you have nothing to do (training paused, event over). The brain occupies itself with rumination.

panels.title: Rumination vs. Reflection
panels.content: Nolen-Hoeksema et al. (2008): rumination doesn't just fail to help — it actively worsens mood, increases depression symptoms, and impairs future problem-solving. People who ruminate after negative events perform WORSE on subsequent challenges. The rumination doesn't just feel bad. It makes you worse.

panels.title: Positive Reflection → Integration → Move On
panels.content: Athletes who completed formal debriefing protocols performed better in subsequent competitions (Thelwell, Greenlees, & Weston, 2006). The debrief wasn't just emotional first aid — it was a learning tool that converted experience into improvement. Without the structured bridge, athletes fall into the gap.

blocks.content: The Post-Race Debrief

steps.title: What went well?
steps.content: Start here. Always. Even if the race was a disaster — especially then. Something went well. You showed up. You deployed the Grit Stack at mile 60. You ate on schedule for three hours. This is deliberate evidence collection for your Evidence File. If you don't catalog what went well, the chimp catalogs what went wrong.

steps.title: What was hard?
steps.content: Not 'what went wrong.' What was HARD. The language matters. 'What went wrong' invites self-blame. 'What was hard' invites honest assessment without judgment. Where did the chimp get loud? Where did your plan break? Neutral observation — the non-judging facet of mindfulness.

steps.title: What would I do differently?
steps.content: Now — and only now — you problem-solve. Not 'I should have been tougher.' What, specifically? 'I would start the Grit Stack earlier.' 'I would add an If-Then for the headwind section.' 'I would add a 5-minute buffer to my pre-race protocol.' Actionable. Specific. Forward-looking.

steps.title: What am I proud of?
steps.content: End here. Always. Not the same as Question 1 — that was analytical. This is emotional. What do you respect about yourself for having done this? This deposits directly into your self-concept. 'I am the kind of athlete who shows up with a plan' is a very different identity entry than 'I always go out too hard.'

blocks.content: The rules: Complete within 24 hours. Write it — not in your head. Thinking about it is rumination; writing is reflection. Then close it. The debrief has a beginning, a middle, and an end.
Commitment: I will complete a written Post-Race Debrief within 24 hours of my next race.
Write it. Close it. You are not your last result.
Module 5 complete. You have the race-day protocol.
Rate yourself again: How structured is your mental race-day preparation? Compare to your score at the start of Module 5.

lessons.title: Module 5 Quiz
lessons.description: Test your understanding of the race-day protocol from night-before through post-race debrief.

blocks.question: Night-Before Checklist: logistics should be finalized by what time?

feedback.correct: Correct. Equipment finalized by 7 PM. When gear is laid out and visible, the chimp can't obsess over uncertainty.
feedback.incorrect: The Night-Before Checklist calls for logistics lockdown by 7 PM — 60-90 minutes before bed — so the brain stops running the 'did I forget something' loop.

blocks.question: Pre-Race Protocol: Min 1-5 = ___, Min 6-8 = ___, Min 9-10 = ___.

options.label: A
options.text: 6-2-7 breathing, Highlight Reel, Schema Statement + Performance Statements

options.label: B
options.text: Highlight Reel, Schema Statement, Activation Calibration

feedback.correct: Correct. Breathing first (physiological foundation), visualization second (builds on calm), identity priming third (loads working memory). Each phase builds on the previous one.
feedback.incorrect: The protocol order matters: Min 1-5 = 6-2-7 breathing reset, Min 6-8 = Highlight Reel visualization, Min 9-10 = Schema Statement + Performance Statements. Don't shuffle them.

blocks.question: In the first hour of a race, you should trust ___ first, and use ___ for confirmation.

options.label: B
options.text: Objective data (watch/power) first, RPE for confirmation

options.label: C
options.text: Your competitor's pace first, RPE for confirmation

options.label: D
options.text: How your legs feel first, heart rate for confirmation

feedback.correct: Correct. Adrenaline subtracts 1-3 RPE points in the first hour, making threshold feel like tempo. Trust data early. Switch to RPE as primary later when adrenaline fades.
feedback.incorrect: In the first hour, adrenaline corrupts RPE — a 7 feels like a 5. Trust objective data (watch, power meter) first, then use RPE for confirmation. Later in the race, the relationship inverts.

blocks.question: Match each Grit Stack step to its action.

options.label: A
options.text: Breathe = 6-2-7 cycle, Detach = 'That's the chimp,' Reframe = Performance Statement, Execute = One technical cue, Segment = Next checkpoint only

options.label: B
options.text: Breathe = Deep breath, Detach = Close eyes, Reframe = Think positive, Execute = Push harder, Segment = Count miles

options.label: C
options.text: Breathe = Slow down, Detach = Ignore pain, Reframe = Visualize finish, Execute = Sprint, Segment = Time-based goals

options.label: D
options.text: Breathe = Box breathing, Detach = Dissociate, Reframe = Mantra, Execute = Increase cadence, Segment = Split remaining distance in half

feedback.correct: Correct. Five specific actions, each doing a specific thing to your brain: parasympathetic brake, cognitive defusion, perceptual reappraisal, instructional self-talk, and task segmentation.
feedback.incorrect: The Grit Stack: Breathe (one 6-2-7 cycle) → Detach ('That's the chimp') → Reframe (deploy your Performance Statement) → Execute (one specific technical cue) → Segment (next checkpoint only, shrink the world).

blocks.question: The Post-Race Debrief should be completed within:

options.label: C
options.text: When you feel ready to process

feedback.correct: Correct. Within 24 hours — emotional memory is still accessible, details are fresh, and the narrative hasn't hardened yet. You can still shape the story.
feedback.incorrect: The Post-Race Debrief must be completed within 24 hours. After that, the narrative hardens. Thinking about it without structure is rumination, not reflection.

blocks.question: 6. Scenario: An athlete rehearsed their nutrition plan three times in training. On race day, a volunteer hands them an unfamiliar gel at an aid station. Their planned gel is in the drop bag at mile 60. They're at mile 40. What should they do?

options.label: A
options.text: Take the unfamiliar gel — fuel is fuel and you need calories now

options.label: B
options.text: Skip nutrition entirely until mile 60 when the drop bag arrives

options.label: C
options.text: Water only until mile 60, then execute the rehearsed plan from the drop bag

options.label: D
options.text: Take the unfamiliar gel and chase it with extra water to reduce GI risk

feedback.correct: Correct. Nothing new on race day is psychological, not just physiological. The unfamiliar gel introduces decision load and GI anxiety. Water bridges the gap. The rehearsed plan resumes at mile 60.
feedback.incorrect: Review Lesson 5.5. 'Nothing new on race day' exists because unfamiliar nutrition adds decision load and anxiety — both of which impair the prefrontal cortex. Water bridges the gap until the rehearsed plan can resume.

blocks.question: Scenario: Mile 50 of a 100-mile race. Your stomach is revolting — you haven't been able to eat for 30 minutes. You've lost 2 positions. The chimp is screaming 'You're going to bonk. This is over.' Deploy the Grit Stack. What is the correct sequence?

options.label: A
options.text: Push harder to catch the riders ahead, then worry about nutrition at the next aid station

options.label: B
options.text: Breathe (one 6-2-7) → Detach ('That's the chimp catastrophizing') → Reframe ('I have fuel for 30 more minutes, next aid is 10 away — this is a hiccup, not a crisis') → Execute ('Sip water, small bites, keep the pedals turning') → Segment ('Next aid station. That's my only job.')

options.label: C
options.text: Stop and rest until your stomach settles, then resume racing

options.label: D
options.text: Dissociate from the pain and think about something else until the discomfort passes

feedback.correct: Correct. The Grit Stack deployed precisely: the breath breaks the panic spiral, detachment separates you from the chimp's catastrophizing, reframing converts 'crisis' to 'hiccup' with actual data, execution gives you one concrete action, and segmentation shrinks the world to the next checkpoint.
feedback.incorrect: In a nutritional crisis, the Grit Stack sequence is: Breathe (break the panic spiral) → Detach ('That's the chimp, not reality') → Reframe (use actual data — 'next aid is 10 minutes away') → Execute (one concrete action: sip, eat small, keep moving) → Segment (next checkpoint only). Dissociating or pushing harder feeds the spiral.

lessons.title: The Emotional Rollercoaster Is Normal
lessons.description: The emotional rollercoaster is not weakness. It's a sign that this mattered to you.

blocks.content: Module 6: When It All Goes Wrong. Adversity is the defining feature. Resilience is a skill.
Before you begin, rate yourself 1-10: How well do you handle setbacks and adversity? Write it down. You'll re-rate at the end of this module.

Six weeks into a training block. You've been nailing workouts. Numbers trending up. You can feel it — this is your year. And then something breaks. A knee that makes you pull up mid-stride. Burnout so slow you didn't see it until you were sitting in your car, engine running, unable to walk into the gym. A relationship falls apart, a job disappears, a family member gets sick. The rug just got pulled.
By the end of this lesson, you will recognize the grief stages in athletic setbacks and be able to name your current stage.
The emotional rollercoaster is not weakness. It's a sign that this mattered to you. The only way through it is to name where you are and stop judging yourself for being on the ride.

events.title: Denial
events.content: "It's not that bad. I'll rest two days and be fine." You keep checking your training plan like it's still relevant. You Google "can you run on a stress fracture" at 11 PM.

events.title: Anger
events.content: "Why me? I did everything right." You're furious at your body, the sport, the universe. You snap at people closest to you because the rage has nowhere else to go.

events.title: Bargaining
events.content: "If I just cross-train, I won't lose any fitness. Maybe I can do the race at 80%." You're negotiating with reality — trying to cut a deal with a situation that doesn't negotiate.

events.title: Depression
events.content: "What's the point?" The quiet one. The alarm goes off and you don't get up. Not because you're tired — because nothing feels worth getting up for.

events.title: Acceptance
events.content: "This is where I am. What can I do from here?" Not happiness. Not being OK with it. You've stopped fighting reality and started working with it.

panels.title: Kubler-Ross (1969) — The Five Stages
panels.content: Elisabeth Kubler-Ross's On Death and Dying established the five stages of grief — denial, anger, bargaining, depression, acceptance. So embedded in culture that people reference them without knowing the source.

panels.title: Rotella & Heyman (1986) — Applied to Sport
panels.content: In Applied Sport Psychology, Rotella and Heyman argued that injured athletes experience genuine grief. When an athlete loses the ability to train and compete, they lose identity, routine, social connection, and goal pursuit. That's a real loss. Real losses produce grief.

panels.title: Heil's Affective Cycle (1993) — Non-Linear
panels.content: John Heil's Psychology of Sport Injury changed the conversation: the stages aren't linear. His Affective Cycle describes three phases — distress, denial, and determined coping — and athletes oscillate between them. Acceptance on Monday, fury on Tuesday, bargaining on Wednesday. That's how this works.

panels.title: Every Experience Is Unique
panels.content: Wiese-Bjornstal's 1998 Integrated Model showed your emotional response is filtered through personal factors (personality, history, how central sport is to your identity) and situational factors (sport, time of season, social support). You can't compare your recovery to anyone else's. Each setback is its own event.

blocks.content: The stages are not linear. You will cycle. You might feel acceptance on Monday and wake up angry on Tuesday. Don't compare to anyone else's timeline.
Name Your Stage

steps.title: Where are you on the rollercoaster?
steps.content: Am I in denial? Angry? Bargaining? Depressed? In determined coping? Pause and ask honestly.

steps.title: Name it
steps.content: Out loud if you can. "I'm angry right now." "I'm bargaining." "I'm in denial and I know it." Naming creates distance — you shift from BEING angry to OBSERVING anger.

steps.title: Write it down
steps.content: Naming kills self-judgment. When what you're feeling has a name that appears in clinical textbooks, you stop beating yourself up for feeling it.

steps.title: No judgment — all stages are normal
steps.content: The injury is enough. The burnout is enough. You don't need to also feel guilty about being upset. Every athlete who has ever been sidelined has felt the same thing.

blocks.prompt: You tore your hamstring two weeks ago. You told your coach "I'll run next week." Your PT says six more weeks. What are you telling yourself?

options.label: A
options.text: "I'm fine, it's not that bad. I've trained through worse."
options.consequence: Denial. You're checking your training plan like it's still relevant and refusing to accept the timeline. The injury doesn't care about your optimism.

options.label: B
options.text: "Maybe I can still race if I cross-train hard enough."
options.consequence: Bargaining. You're negotiating with reality, trying to cut a deal with a situation that doesn't negotiate. The math doesn't work, but the brain keeps running the numbers.

options.label: C
options.text: "I'm injured. This is where I am right now. What can I do from here?"
options.consequence: Acceptance. Not happiness — clarity. You've stopped fighting reality and started working with it. This is where recovery begins.

blocks.content: My current stage is: ___. I accept that this is where I am right now.
Name where you are. Stop judging yourself for being on the ride.
Next — stress isn't just a feeling. It's in your muscles, your sleep, your immune system, and your recovery timeline.

lessons.title: Stress Is Physical
lessons.description: Mental stress amplifies physical pain. The vicious cycle is real.

blocks.content: Notice something right now. Where are your shoulders — up by your ears or dropped? Is your jaw clenched? Take one breath. Was it shallow, chest-only? If your shoulders were high, your jaw was tight, and your breath was shallow — you're stressed. And you probably didn't know it until you looked.

Stress isn't just a feeling. It's a physiological state. It lives in your muscles, your sleep, your digestion, your pain perception. When you're going through a setback, the stress doesn't stay in the "mental" category. Your body doesn't distinguish between "I'm stressed about my injury" and "a bear is chasing me." The machinery is the same.
By the end of this lesson, you will have completed a 5-domain Stress Audit and identified where stress is leaking between domains.

blocks.question: Heil's Affective Cycle of injury response is best described as:

options.label: A
options.text: A linear progression from denial to acceptance

options.label: B
options.text: Non-linear — athletes cycle and oscillate between stages

options.label: C
options.text: A fixed 5-stage model identical to Kubler-Ross

options.label: D
options.text: A model that applies only to career-ending injuries

feedback.correct: Right. Heil showed that athletes oscillate between distress, denial, and determined coping. Acceptance on Monday, anger on Tuesday. That's the cycle.
feedback.incorrect: Not quite. Heil's key insight was that the stages are non-linear — athletes cycle between distress, denial, and determined coping. There's no neat progression.

blocks.content: Mental stress amplifies physical pain. Stress tightens muscles, steals sleep, elevates cortisol, increases inflammation, and makes every session feel harder than it is. The vicious cycle is real.

tabs.label: Stress Relievers (Acute)
tabs.content: Work in the moment. Music. Laughter. Time in nature. A phone call with someone who makes you feel seen. Unplugging from social media for 24 hours. These are aspirin for the headache — they don't solve the underlying problem, but they give your nervous system a break so it can function.

tabs.label: Stress Balancers (Structural)
tabs.content: Address the system. Sleep hygiene. Regular social connection. Meditation or mindfulness. Journaling. Consistent physical activity within your current limitations. These take longer, but they change the baseline. They lower the waterline so daily stressors don't overflow as easily.

panels.title: The Sympathetic Cascade
panels.content: Williams and Andersen's 1998 Stress-Athletic Injury Model found that athletes carrying high life stress responded to competitive stressors with greater physiological arousal — increased muscle tension, narrowed attention, decreased peripheral vision. Stress didn't just make them feel bad. It changed how their bodies functioned and directly increased injury probability.

panels.title: The Vicious Cycle: Stress → Sleep → Stress → Recovery
panels.content: Fullagar's 2015 review in Sports Medicine laid it bare: sleep deprivation elevates cortisol, impairs glycogen resynthesis, reduces reaction time, increases perceived effort, and decreases motivation. Stress disrupts sleep. Poor sleep raises cortisol. Elevated cortisol increases inflammation and pain. You feel worse, which adds more stress. The cycle doesn't break itself. You have to intervene.

blocks.content: The Stress Audit

steps.title: Rate 1-10 across five domains
steps.content: Training/Sport, Work/School, Relationships, Sleep, Health. 1 = peaceful and stable. 10 = on fire and barely coping. Be honest.

steps.title: Find the leak
steps.content: Add up all five. Over 30? You're carrying a stress load that's almost certainly affecting performance, recovery, and health. Now find the highest single score — that's your leak. A 9 in relationships doesn't stay in relationships — it raises training stress, disrupts sleep, affects health. Stress bleeds between domains.

steps.title: Deploy stress relievers for acute relief
steps.content: Grab the quick hits to get through the day: music, nature, a call with someone who gets it, unplugging from social media. These don't solve the root cause, but they give your nervous system a break.

steps.title: Deploy stress balancers for the root cause
steps.content: Target the highest-scoring domain with a specific structural change. Sleep highest? No screens after 9 PM, 6-2-7 in bed, consistent wake time. Work highest? Reinstate one boundary this week. Relationships? Have the conversation you've been avoiding.

blocks.content: I will complete the Stress Audit today and address the highest-scoring domain this week.
Stress doesn't stay in its lane. Find the leak. Fix the leak.
Next — coping strategies matched to what you can control.

lessons.title: Coping — What Actually Works
lessons.description: Problem-focused when you can control it. Emotion-focused when you can't. Avoidance always backfires.

blocks.content: Two athletes. Same injury — tibial stress reaction, eight weeks off, same prognosis. Athlete A scrolls social media, sees training partners posting long runs, gets furious. Tests the leg against doctor's orders. Ignores the pain. Tells nobody. Lies in bed replaying the moment of injury until 2 AM. Sleep: four hours.

Athlete B feels the same frustration. Names it: "I'm angry today. That's OK." Journals three things she's frustrated about, then writes one thing she can control: her rehab appointment. Goes. Asks specific questions. Calls a friend. Sets a short-term goal. Bed at 10. Sleep: seven hours. Same injury. Wildly different trajectories. The difference is strategy.
By the end of this lesson, you will be able to match coping strategy to situation based on controllability.

blocks.question: The Stress Audit evaluates how many domains?

feedback.correct: Right. Training/Sport, Work/School, Relationships, Sleep, and Health. Find the highest score — that's the leak.
feedback.incorrect: The Stress Audit rates five domains: Training/Sport, Work/School, Relationships, Sleep, and Health.

blocks.content: Problem-focused when you can control it. Emotion-focused when you can't. Avoidance always backfires.

tabs.label: Problem-Focused
tabs.content: Take action to change the situation. Make a plan. Seek information. Adjust goals. DO something about the problem. Best when the stressor is controllable — concrete actions are available. The athlete who calls the PT, researches alternative races, builds a cross-training plan.

tabs.label: Emotion-Focused
tabs.content: Manage the feelings the situation produces. Talk to someone. Journal. Practice mindfulness. Give yourself compassion. Process the emotion rather than fixing the unfixable. Best when the stressor is outside your control. You can't change it, so you manage your relationship with it.

tabs.label: Avoidance
tabs.content: Deny, distract, or dodge. Pretend it's not happening. Numb out with screens, food, alcohol, overwork. Scrolling numbs anxiety for 20 minutes. Telling yourself "it's fine" keeps panic at bay for a day. But everything you delay compounds. Avoidance is debt with interest. It always costs more than the original bill.

blocks.question: Sort each scenario into Problem-Focused or Emotion-Focused coping:

items.text: Bad weather forecast for race day
Recurring knee pain you haven't had examined
Got passed in the final km — can't change it now
GI issues caused by your current nutrition plan

feedback.correct: Exactly. The question is always: can I control this? If yes, problem-focused. If no, emotion-focused.
feedback.incorrect: The key question: can I control this? Weather and past race moments — no (emotion-focused). Knee pain and nutrition plans — yes (problem-focused).

panels.title: Lazarus & Folkman (1984) — The Transactional Model
panels.content: Stress, Appraisal, and Coping established the framework. Stress isn't just about the event — it's the transaction between person and event. Primary appraisal: "Is this a threat?" Secondary appraisal: "Can I handle it?" Your coping response follows from those two assessments. Nicholls and Polman (2007) confirmed the pattern across multiple sports: problem-focused strategies worked better when the stressor was controllable; avoidance was consistently linked to greater anxiety.

panels.title: Self-Compassion as Coping (Mosewich, 2011)
panels.content: Mosewich found that self-compassion isn't just an identity tool — it's a coping mechanism. Treating yourself with the same kindness you'd offer a struggling training partner buffered against self-criticism after failures. It didn't lower standards. It prevented adding a second layer of suffering on top of the first. The injury hurts. Self-criticism makes the injury AND your mental state worse. Self-compassion addresses the mental state while the injury heals.

blocks.content: The Coping Match

steps.title: Name your biggest stressor
steps.content: Be specific. Not "my injury" — "I have a stress reaction in my tibia and I can't run for eight weeks." Not "everything sucks" — name the actual thing.

steps.title: Can you control it?
steps.content: Not "can I influence it a little" or "can I wish really hard." Can you take concrete action that will materially change the situation?

steps.title: If yes: 3 action steps
steps.content: Write three specific actions for this week. Not goals — actions. "Call the PT." "Research alternative races." "Build a cross-training plan." Then do the first one. Today.

steps.title: If no: acceptance + self-compassion
steps.content: Name it. 6-2-7 breathing to calm the physiology. Teammate Test to check your narrative. Then the slower work — talking to someone, journaling, sitting with discomfort. Triage sequence: name, breathe, reframe.

steps.title: Watch for avoidance
steps.content: Are you "problem-solving" something uncontrollable? That's avoidance in a productive mask. "Processing emotions" about something you could fix with one phone call? Avoidance in a therapeutic mask. If you've been "coping" for two weeks and nothing has changed — you might be avoiding.

blocks.content: My biggest stressor right now is ___. I [can/cannot] control it. My strategy is ___.
Match the strategy to the situation. Problem-focused when you can act. Emotion-focused when you can't. Stop adding self-punishment on top of suffering.
Next — the uninvited teacher: injury.

lessons.title: Injury — The Uninvited Teacher
lessons.description: Injury threatens identity. If the athlete branch is the whole tree, losing it is existential.

blocks.content: There's a specific moment every injured athlete knows. Not the pop or the snap — that's adrenaline and denial. The real moment comes later. The doctor's office. The PT drawing a timeline. Or the first morning when your alarm goes off at training time and you have no reason to get up. The routine that structured your entire day — gone.

"I am injured" is an identity statement — it makes the injury who you are. "I am dealing with an injury" is a situation statement — it makes the injury something you're navigating. Same facts. Different framing. That framing shapes everything.
By the end of this lesson, you will have a ranked Return Hierarchy for your fears about getting back.

blocks.question: Match each coping style to the correct situation:

pairs.right: Recurring knee pain you can get examined
Bad weather on race day you can't change
Scrolling social media to numb anxiety about injury

feedback.correct: Right. Problem-focused for controllable situations, emotion-focused for uncontrollable ones, and avoidance always backfires — it's debt with interest.
feedback.incorrect: Problem-focused when you can act, emotion-focused when you can't, and avoidance is always a disguised delay that compounds.

blocks.content: Injury threatens identity. If the athlete branch is the whole tree, losing it is existential.
Cross-reference: Identity Foreclosure (Lesson 2.1) — athletes with diverse identity branches recover faster from injury. One branch thinning doesn't topple the tree.

events.title: Sadness Peaks Early
events.content: You've just lost something — routine, goals, identity. The grief is immediate and appropriate.

events.title: Anxiety Peaks at Return
events.content: Counterintuitively, the hardest psychological phase comes when you're getting BACK. Fear of re-injury is the most commonly reported barrier (Podlog & Eklund, 2007).

events.title: Fear Creates Physical Tension
events.content: Fear of re-injury causes muscle tension. Tension alters movement patterns. Altered movement changes biomechanics. The psychology becomes the physiology.

events.title: Tension Increases Re-Injury Risk
events.content: Changed biomechanics increase actual injury risk. The runner with the compensatory limp. The cyclist who tenses in every group sprint. The tissue healed. The brain didn't.

panels.title: Brewer 1994 — Athletic Identity Foreclosure
panels.content: Athletes who define themselves solely through sport experience more severe psychological responses to injury. The Athletic Identity Measurement Scale showed a direct correlation between identity narrowness and depression post-injury.

panels.title: Podlog & Eklund 2007 — Fear of Reinjury
panels.content: Fear of reinjury is the most commonly cited barrier to return. Athletes who returned before psychologically ready showed higher reinjury rates and lower performance satisfaction.

panels.title: Walker, Thatcher & Lavallee 2007 — Cognitive Appraisal
panels.content: An athlete's psychological response to injury depends on how central sport is to their identity, not the severity of the injury itself.

cards.back: Someone who understands the emotional rollercoaster and validates it. Not fixes — validates. Most injured athletes are missing this one.
Proper medical care, competent rehabilitation, progressive return-to-activity planning. The body-fixing side.
Clear answers to clear questions. Realistic timelines. Honest assessments. "What CAN I do? What does progress look like?"
Someone who reminds you that you WILL come back, that the work matters, that the process is worth it even when it doesn't feel like it.

blocks.content: The Return Hierarchy

steps.title: List your fears
steps.content: Every one. Don't filter or rank yet. "I'm afraid the pain will come back." "I'm afraid I've lost all my fitness." "I'm afraid I'll look slow." "I'm afraid this will happen again." Dump them all out.

steps.title: Rank least to most scary
steps.content: Put the fear that makes you wince at the top. The one that makes your stomach drop at the bottom. Create a ladder.

steps.title: Address in order — start small
steps.content: Take the least scary fear first. What's one thing you can do this week to confront it? A controlled fitness test. A graduated return session at 50%. One rung at a time. Don't jump to the worst one.

steps.title: Each conquered fear = Evidence File entry
steps.content: Don't just confront the fear — collect evidence. Write it down: "I did a 30-min easy session and pain was 2/10 and gone within an hour." Each rung adds a brick to the Evidence File from Lesson 2.2. Proof the chimp was catastrophizing.

blocks.content: Cross-reference: Add each conquered fear to your Evidence File from Lesson 2.2. Earned confidence overwrites catastrophic narratives.
My top 3 return fears, ranked: 1.___ 2.___ 3.___
The Return Hierarchy breaks the massive fear of coming back into small, sequential, evidence-building steps. One rung at a time.
Next — what mental toughness actually is. Hint: it includes knowing when to stop.

lessons.title: Mental Toughness Is the Sum
lessons.description: Mental toughness is the integration of every tool in this course.

blocks.content: The toughest athlete I ever coached quit a race on purpose. She was 60 miles into a 100-miler, running third in her age group, when she felt something shift in her knee. She assessed it for two miles. Pulled her drop bag. Drove home. An MRI confirmed early meniscus damage. She won that event the following year. The woman who finished in her place tore her ACL at mile 85 and missed two seasons.

Mental toughness is not "pushing through pain." It's not ignoring your body. It's not a death wish dressed in athletic clothing. The triathlete who destroyed herself in visible medical distress, collapsed at the finish, and spent two days in the hospital — everyone called her "tough." She wasn't tough. She was reckless. Mental toughness is a collection of trained, practiced, developed skills. And it includes knowing when to stop.
By the end of this lesson, you will have rated yourself on the 4 C's and identified which module to revisit.

blocks.question: Select ALL four types of support an injured athlete needs (Cheadle):

feedback.correct: All four. Psychological, Physical, Informational, Motivational. Most injured athletes get one or two. The missing ones are usually psychological and motivational.
feedback.incorrect: The four types are: Psychological (validation), Physical (medical care/rehab), Informational (clear answers, realistic timelines), and Motivational (reminders you WILL come back).

blocks.content: Mental toughness is the integration of every tool in this course. Breathing, identity, visualization, self-talk, coping, execution. None alone is mental toughness. All together, deployed at the right times, IS mental toughness.

blocks.alt: Clough's 4 C's model — Control, Commitment, Challenge, Confidence — with self-belief at the center hub
blocks.caption: Clough's 4 C's of Mental Toughness. Zeiger's Sisu study found 12 dimensions radiating from a single hub: self-belief. Without it, everything wobbles.

panels.title: Clough's 4 C's Model (2002)
panels.content: Built on Kobasa's hardiness research. Control: belief you can influence outcomes and manage emotional response. Commitment: purposeful persistence aligned with values. Challenge: seeing adversity as opportunity, not threat. Confidence: evidence-based belief in your ability to perform.

panels.title: Zeiger's Sisu Study — Self-Belief at Center
panels.content: Joanna Zeiger's study of endurance athletes found 12 dimensions of mental toughness, all radiating from a single hub: self-belief. Without it, everything wobbles. Self-belief isn't empty confidence — it's earned from training, past performances, and accumulated evidence.

panels.title: Meta-Analyses: MT Is Trainable
panels.content: Stamatis et al. (2020, BMJ Open Sport & Exercise Medicine): structured psychological skill interventions produce LARGE effects on mental toughness — not marginal, measurable. Gucciardi et al. (2015, Journal of Personality): mental toughness is context-dependent and developable through deliberate training. Full stop.

panels.title: MT = Integration of All Tools
panels.content: When you catch the chimp mid-spiral and deploy a performance statement — that's MT. When you breathe your heart rate down on a start line and execute your plan — that's MT. When you get injured and name your stage, do the Coping Match, and build a Return Hierarchy — that's MT. A thousand small, skilled decisions under pressure.

blocks.content: The MT Self-Assessment

steps.title: Rate 1-10 on each C
steps.content: Control: Can I manage my emotional response, or does the chimp run the show? Commitment: Do I show up consistently when nobody's watching? Challenge: Do I see adversity as growth or punishment? Confidence: Do I believe in my ability based on evidence? Be brutally honest.

steps.title: Find your weakest C
steps.content: Look at your four numbers. Which is lowest? That's your target. That's the link in the chain that breaks under pressure.

steps.title: Go to the module that builds it
steps.content: Control → Module 1 (Gap Check, 6-2-7, Know Your Zone). Commitment → Module 4 (Motivation Check, Off-Day Protocol) + Schema Statement. Challenge → Module 3 (If-Then Plans, Disaster Planning). Confidence → Module 2 (Evidence File, Schema Statement, Performance Statements).

steps.title: One tool, five days
steps.content: Pick one tool from the target module. Practice it every day for five days. If Confidence scored a 4, add to your Evidence File for five days. If Control scored a 5, do 6-2-7 before every session. Then reassess. Watch the number move.

blocks.content: Module Map — Control: Module 1. Confidence: Module 2. Challenge: Module 3. Commitment: Module 4.
Most athletes completing this course find 1-2 C's noticeably weaker than the others. That's normal. The goal isn't perfection — it's knowing which link to reinforce.
My weakest C is ___. I will practice [specific tool] for 5 days.
Rate yourself. Find the weak link. Go build it.
Next — the final lesson: the habit, not the goal.

lessons.title: The Habit, Not The Goal
lessons.description: Daily micro-practice beats weekly deep sessions. The tools work — but only if you use them.

blocks.content: Six modules. Thirty lessons. More tools than you can fit in a toolbox. You probably feel two things: "I get the framework" and "There's no way I'll remember all this." The second feeling is more honest. And it's fine. You don't need to remember all of it. You need to pick up the habit. Not the goal. The habit.
By the end of this lesson, you will have a Daily 5 routine and commit to starting tonight.

blocks.question: Match each of the 4 C's to the module that builds it:

feedback.correct: Exactly. Control → M1 (Gap Check, 6-2-7). Confidence → M2 (Evidence File). Challenge → M3 (If-Then Plans). Commitment → M4 (Motivation Check).
feedback.incorrect: Control → Module 1. Confidence → Module 2. Challenge → Module 3. Commitment → Module 4. Each module builds the skills that feed that specific C.

blocks.content: Daily micro-practice beats weekly deep sessions. Five minutes daily beats 35 minutes weekly. The tools work — but only if you use them.

events.title: Stage 1: Unconscious Incompetence
events.content: You don't know what you don't know. Before this course, you didn't know about the chimp and the coach, the central governor, attentional flexibility. Operating without an owner's manual.

events.title: Stage 2: Conscious Incompetence
events.content: You know what you should be doing, but you're bad at it. The 6-2-7 feels awkward. You forget performance statements in races. You SEE the errors — which makes it feel like you're getting WORSE. This is the danger zone where most people quit.

events.title: Stage 3: Conscious Competence
events.content: You can do it, but it requires effort. You use the 6-2-7 on the start line and it works, but you have to deliberately remember. You catch the chimp mid-spiral, but it takes real energy. It works. It's just not automatic yet.

events.title: Stage 4: Unconscious Competence
events.content: It's automatic. You breathe through a race crisis without deciding to. Performance statements fire on their own. You're not DOING mental skills — you ARE mentally skilled. The tools are part of the operating system.

blocks.content: Stage 2 feels like getting worse. That's awareness outpacing skill — you can see the errors now, which is the prerequisite for fixing them. Expect it. Push through it.

panels.title: 5 Minutes Daily > 30 Minutes Weekly
panels.content: Ericsson's deliberate practice research (1993): expert performance requires spaced, focused practice. Short, frequent, intentional sessions are the fastest path from "I know this" to "I do this automatically." The brain consolidates skills through spacing, not cramming.

panels.title: Habit Formation Research
panels.content: Lally et al. (2010, European Journal of Social Psychology): habit formation takes 18-254 days, median 66. Exercise behaviors take 1.5x longer to automatize than eating or drinking habits. Mental habits should be easier — but only if you start. And only if you keep going past the point where it stops being novel.

panels.title: The Competence Valley
panels.content: The MAC approach (Gardner & Moore, 2004) confirmed it: daily mindfulness and acceptance practice, maintained as a habit, produced performance gains. Not occasional practice. Not pre-race-only. Daily, over time. Without the habit, skills decay. With the habit, skills compound.

blocks.content: The Daily 5

steps.title: Minute 1: Breathe (6-2-7)
steps.content: Four cycles. Your physiological reset. Activates the parasympathetic brake, anchors you in the present, creates the calm foundation for everything that follows.

steps.title: Minute 2: Highlight Reel
steps.content: One vivid scene — empowering past moment or crucial future moment. Ten seconds of full sensory detail, first person, full immersion. You're priming the pump for confidence and self-efficacy.

steps.title: Minute 3: Performance Statements
steps.content: Say your three statements — technical, pain response, process — silently or out loud. Repeat each several times. This is self-talk programming. Loading the ammunition before the chimp shows up.

steps.title: Minute 4: Schema Statement
steps.content: Who you are. What you want. The strength and the aspiration. Don't just recite it — mean it. Identity maintenance that keeps the "I am" statement fresh and active.

steps.title: Minute 5: Gratitude + Reflection
steps.content: Name one specific thing you're grateful for as an athlete today. Then one question: What's one thing I'm going to do well today? Grounds you in the present. Sends you into the day with direction.

blocks.content: Start tonight. Not next Monday. Tonight. Five minutes before bed. By day three, you'll have it memorized. By day seven, you stop reading the cards. The statements are yours.
I will start The Daily 5 tonight at [time].
The tools in this course work. But only if you use them.

Six modules. You've got the science, the tools, the framework. You'll notice the difference the first time you hit a wall and reach for a tool instead of an excuse. Five minutes a day. Not a goal — a habit.
Rate yourself again: How well do you handle setbacks and adversity? Compare to your Module 6 opening score.

lessons.title: Module 6 Quiz
lessons.description: Test your understanding of adversity, coping, injury, mental toughness, and habit formation.

blocks.question: Match each grief stage to the athlete example:

pairs.right: "I'll rest two days and be fine. I've trained through worse."
"Why me? I did everything right. This isn't fair."
"If I just cross-train, I won't lose any fitness."
"What's the point? Everyone else will be ahead of me."
"This is where I am. What can I do from here?"

feedback.correct: Right. Naming the stage creates distance between you and the emotion — from BEING angry to OBSERVING anger.
feedback.incorrect: Denial = pretending it's not that bad. Anger = fury at the unfairness. Bargaining = negotiating with reality. Depression = withdrawing and losing motivation. Acceptance = working with reality, not fighting it.

blocks.question: Sort each coping scenario into Problem-Focused or Emotion-Focused:

items.text: Journaling about frustration after a DNF
Using the Teammate Test after a bad workout
Adjusting nutrition plan to fix GI issues
Practicing 6-2-7 breathing after getting race results you can't change

feedback.correct: Exactly. Problem-focused when you can take concrete action. Emotion-focused when the situation is outside your control and you manage your relationship with it.
feedback.incorrect: Problem-focused = taking action to change the situation (PT sessions, alternative races, nutrition adjustments). Emotion-focused = managing feelings about what you can't change (journaling, Teammate Test, breathing after fixed results).

blocks.question: Select ALL four types of support an injured athlete needs (Cheadle):

options.label: A
options.text: Psychological support — validation of the emotional experience

options.label: B
options.text: Physical support — proper medical care and rehabilitation

options.label: C
options.text: Informational support — clear answers, realistic timelines

options.label: E
options.text: Motivational support — reminders you will come back

feedback.correct: All four: Psychological, Physical, Informational, Motivational. Most injured athletes get one or two — the missing ones are usually psychological and motivational.
feedback.incorrect: The four types: Psychological (validation), Physical (medical care/rehab), Informational (clear timelines/honest assessment), Motivational (someone reminding you the work matters).

blocks.question: Match each of the 4 C's to the module that builds it:

pairs.right: Module 1 — Gap Check, 6-2-7, Know Your Zone

feedback.correct: Each C maps to a module. Find the weakest. Go build it. One tool, five days.
feedback.incorrect: Control → M1 (physiological regulation). Confidence → M2 (identity and evidence). Challenge → M3 (visualization and preparation). Commitment → M4 (motivation and persistence).

blocks.question: Complete the Daily 5 sequence: Minute 1 = ___, Minute 2 = ___, Minute 3 = ___, Minute 4 = ___, Minute 5 = ___.

feedback.correct: Breathe. See. Speak. Affirm. Reflect. Five minutes. Every day.
feedback.incorrect: The Daily 5: Min 1 = 6-2-7 Breathing. Min 2 = Highlight Reel. Min 3 = Performance Statements. Min 4 = Schema Statement. Min 5 = Gratitude and Reflection.

blocks.question: Stage 2 of the competence model (Conscious Incompetence) feels like:

options.label: B
options.text: Getting worse — you can see errors you couldn't see before

feedback.correct: Right. Awareness has outpaced skill. You can see the errors now, which feels terrible — but seeing them is the prerequisite for fixing them. This is where most people quit. Don't.
feedback.incorrect: Stage 2 feels like getting worse. You know what you should be doing but you're bad at it. The 6-2-7 feels awkward, you forget statements in races. That's awareness outpacing skill — and it's exactly where growth happens.

blocks.prompt: An athlete is 4 weeks post-surgery on a meniscus repair. Physical therapy is going well. When asked how they're doing, they say: "I'm fine. I just need to push through it. I'll be racing again in two weeks." Their PT says the timeline is 8-10 more weeks.

options.label: A
options.text: This athlete is in the Denial stage and should use problem-focused coping to speed up recovery.
options.consequence: Half right. The stage identification is correct — denial. But problem-focused coping aimed at accelerating an uncontrollable timeline is exactly how athletes re-injure themselves. The healing timeline isn't controllable.

options.label: B
options.text: This athlete is in the Denial stage and should use emotion-focused coping — name the stage, practice acceptance, and lean on their support system while following the medical timeline.
options.consequence: Correct. The stage is denial — "I'm fine" and an unrealistic timeline are textbook markers. The healing process is not controllable, so emotion-focused coping is appropriate: name the stage, accept the real timeline, use the Teammate Test, and ensure they have all four support types (psychological, physical, informational, motivational).

options.label: C
options.text: This athlete is showing mental toughness by staying positive and should be encouraged to push toward their two-week goal.
options.consequence: Dangerous. Confusing denial with toughness is how athletes re-injure themselves. Mental toughness includes knowing when to stop, following medical guidance, and working WITH reality — not overriding it with willpower. Pushing a 10-week rehab into 2 weeks isn't toughness. It's recklessness.

blocks.question: 7. Which of the following correctly lists the 4 C's of mental toughness?

feedback.correct: Correct. Clough's 4 C's: Control (life and emotions), Commitment (goal-setting and persistence), Challenge (seeing threat as opportunity), and Confidence (self-belief and interpersonal). These aren't personality traits — they're trainable skills.
feedback.incorrect: Review Lesson 6.5. The 4 C's from Clough's Mental Toughness model are Control, Commitment, Challenge, and Confidence. Each is a trainable skill, not a fixed trait.
